mainmatter.p: A category is defined to be a univalent precategory.
A precategory C is specified by the following data:

ol.li: a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert of objects, written c \in C;
for each x,y \in C a set C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} of arrows, written f \colon x \to y;
for each x \in C, an identity arrow 1_{ x } \colon x \to x;
for each f \colon x \to y and g \colon y \to z, a composite arrow g \circ f \colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows:
f \circ 1_{ x } = f \quad 1_{ y } \circ f = f \quad h \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathopen {} \left ( h \circ g \right ) \mathclose {} \circ f

mainmatter.p: A functor F \colon C \to D from a precategory C to a precategory D is defined by the following data:

ol.li: a function \left \lvert F \right \rvert \colon \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert D \right \rvert giving the action of F on objects, where we shall write Fx for \left \lvert F \right \rvert x given x \in C;

for each x,y \in C a function F_{x,y} \colon C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \to D \mathopen {} \left ( Fx , Fy \right ) \mathclose {} giving the action of F on arrows, where we shall write Ff \colon Fx \to Fy for F_{x,y}f given f \colon x \to y;

such that the action on morphisms commutes with identities and compositions:
F 1_{ x } = 1_{ Fx } \quad F \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} = Fg \circ Ff

mainmatter.p: A functor F \colon C \to D is called (full, faithful) when the action on arrows is valued in (surjections, embeddings) respectively. It is common to refer to a full and faithful functor as fully faithful.
Fullness and faithfulness are both properties of a functor.
A functor F \colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f \colon C \cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } } {D \cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{ C } \colon C \cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C is a proposition, which we depict in the language of telescopes below:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function between sets that is both an embedding and a surjection is an equivalence. We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:

By the univalence principle for types, we may contract \mathopen {} \left ( D_0,F_0 \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( \left \lvert C \right \rvert , 1_{ \left \lvert C \right \rvert } \right ) \mathclose {}:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types:
By the univalence principle for functions, the telescope above contracts to \mathopen {} \left [ \, \right ] \mathclose {}.

mainmatter.p: Let C and D be precategories, and let F,G \colon C \to D be two functors from C to D. A natural transformation \alpha \colon F \to G is given by a family of arrows \alpha _ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \right ) \mathclose {} } } D \mathopen {} \left ( Fx , Gx \right ) \mathclose {} equipped with a (necessarily unique) naturality datum \alpha _f \colon \alpha _y \circ Ff = Gf \circ \alpha _x for each f \colon x \to y.
A section-retraction pair in a precategory C consists of an arrow s \colon x \to y called the section and an arrow r \colon y \to x called the retraction such that r \circ s = 1_{ x }.

An arrow f \colon x \to y in a precategory C is called an isomorphism when either of there exists an inverse to f, i.e. an arrow g \colon y \to x that is simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
We shall write f \colon x \cong y to denote an isomorphism from x to y.

A precategory C is called univalent when for any x \in C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } y \cong x of isomorphisms onto x is contractible.
The material on univalent category theory is drawn primarily from the HoTT Book and Ahrens, Kapulkin, and Shulman.
A precategory C is specified by the following data:

ol.li: a type \left \lvert C \right \rvert of objects, written c \in C;
for each x,y \in C a set C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} of arrows, written f \colon x \to y;
for each x \in C, an identity arrow 1_{ x } \colon x \to x;
for each f \colon x \to y and g \colon y \to z, a composite arrow g \circ f \colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows:
f \circ 1_{ x } = f \quad 1_{ y } \circ f = f \quad h \circ \mathopen {} \left ( g \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathopen {} \left ( h \circ g \right ) \mathclose {} \circ f

mainmatter.p: A precategory C is called univalent when for any x \in C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } y \cong x of isomorphisms onto x is contractible.
A displayed category over the terminal category gives precisely the data of a category.

The purpose of displayed categories is to define category-like objects that depend on other categories. We will later on learn how to impose conditions that make displayed categories behave like (potentially large) categories internal to other categories. In order to make the theory sufficiently flexible, however, it is necessary to first relativise the notion of displayed category to allow the base to be another (relative) displayed category rather than an ordinary category. The starting point of our approach to relativisation is the insight that a category is a special case of displayed category.
A displayed category over the terminal category gives precisely the data of a category.

In light of the observation above, the base of a displayed category in the sense that we have described so far is another displayed category — whose base is the terminal category. This suggests a simple way to expand our vocabulary to account for displayed categories whose base is another displayed category.

Let B \vdash C displayed precategory over a precategory B. A relative displayed precategory over C, written C \vdash E, is defined to be a displayed precategory B.C \vdash E over the total precategory B.C. A relative displayed category over C is then a displayed category over B.C.
When it is clear from context what we mean, we will speak of displayed categories over C, as in what follows, the relative version of displayed categories will be the primary one.

frontmatter.title: The relative displayed precategory of slices

mainmatter.p: Let B \vdash C be a displayed precategory; we may define a relative displayed precategory C \vdash C _{/} as follows:

ol.li: A displayed object b.c.p \in C _{/} is given by a vertical arrow over b \in B with codomain b.c, i.e. an arrow p \colon \partial _0 p \to c in the fiber precategory b.C.

Given displayed objects b_0.c_0.p_0, b_1.c_1.p_1 \in C _{/}, a displayed arrow i.f. \alpha \colon b_0.c_0.p_0 \to b_1.c_1.p_1 in C _{/} is given by a displayed arrow i. \alpha \colon b_0. \partial _0p_0 \to b_1. \partial _0 p_1 in B.C making the following square commute:
For a displayed object b.c.p \in C _{/}, the displayed identity arrow 1_{ b } . 1_{ c } . 1_{ p } \colon b.c.p \to b.c.p is given by the following square:

The displayed composite of two displayed arrows i.f. \alpha \colon b_0.c_0.p_0 \to b_1.c_1.p_1 and j.g. \beta \colon b_1.c_1.p_1 \to b_2.c_2.p_2 is given by the upstairs displayed composite in B.C depicted below:
